experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [1,2] excludes known evil players and includes me, making it a strong round-1 team. Approving helps Good secure an early success while revealing little beyond support for a clean team.
Player 3: I know Player 4 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [1,2] contains no evil players. On quest 1, a clean team is likely to pass and gives the good side an early success. As evil in the team voting phase, I want to avoid approving all-good teams when possible. Rejecting also reveals little beyond a normal cautious round-1 stance, so it does not significantly hurt evil.
Player 0: I have no concrete role information yet beyond myself being Good. On round 1, quest 1 with a 2-person team, approving is generally standard because there is little evidence and rejecting gives little information while risking early stall. Team [1,2] excludes me, but that alone is not enough to reject. My vote reveals I am open to gathering information from the first quest result, which helps Good more than an arbitrary first-round reject.
Player 1: No role-specific knowledge yet beyond my own: as a Servant, I know only that I am Good and have no confirmed reads on others. It's round 1 on a 2-person quest, and the proposed team includes me as leader plus Player 2. Early in 5-player Avalon, approving an initial team with myself on it is standard because it has a reasonable chance to succeed and rejecting gives little information. My vote reveals mild confidence in my own inclusion but does not significantly aid Evil.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; players 1 and 2 are both non-visible and could be good. On round 1 with quest 1 requiring only one fail, an all-unknown team of [1,2] is likely good-heavy and excludes both evil players, so from evil perspective it's optimal to reject and try to get an evil onto the mission later. My vote itself reveals little this early, and rejecting a two-good team helps evil more than approving a likely successful quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Quest 1 team [1,2] succeeded, so players 1 and 2 are somewhat more likely good, though one evil could have hidden. As a Servant, I want to approve reasonably strong teams that include successful prior members. Team [0,1,2] adds me to the successful pair and is a solid low-risk quest 2 proposal. Approving reveals little individually and supports a team with good evidence.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 1 are the only non-seen players and at least one could be good; the proposed team [0,1,2] excludes both known evil players and includes me. Quest 1 succeeded with [1,2], making 1 somewhat better than random, and adding 0 is reasonable for Quest 2. As good Merlin, approving a clean team is optimal; rejecting would only delay a likely good mission and create unnecessary suspicion. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for a strong team.
Player 4: I am Minion with teammate 3, so team [0,1,2] contains no evil players and should likely succeed if approved. Previous team [1,2] also had no evil and succeeded, making 1 and 2 look somewhat good; 0 is still unknown. As evil, on a 3-person team without evil presence, optimal play is usually to reject to avoid giving Good a free success and to keep pressure for teams including 3 or 4 later. My vote reveals limited info since evil can blend with skeptical good players rejecting an information-poor team.
Player 1: I know the previous successful team was [1,2], which gives some weak positive signal on players 1 and 2, though a 2-person success can still include an evil who chose pass. Player 0 is less cleared, but expanding from a successful core by adding one new player is generally reasonable on quest 2. As a good Servant in team voting, the optimal play is to approve teams that contain the successful prior team and only one unknown, since this can gather information without being overly risky. Approving reveals I am consistent with trusting the successful quest core; rejecting would be unnecessarily conservative and less helpful for good.
Player 3: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1,2] contains no evil and would guarantee a quest success if approved, moving Good to 2-0. The previous successful 2-person team [1,2] gives some cover to 1 and 2 as likely good, though it's not conclusive because evil could have passed. As evil Assassin, in team voting I want to prevent clean all-good teams from going through, especially early, and avoid helping Good build trust chains. Rejecting also aligns with wanting at least one evil on the team eventually; my vote itself reveals little because mixed votes are normal.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know player 3 is Evil. The proposed team [1,3] contains a known Evil player, so from Good's perspective this team should be rejected. Since quest 3 on 5 players needs 2 members, approving a team with known Evil risks a fail and also gives no useful cover benefit here because we already have two clean successes involving players 1 and 2. Rejecting minimizes immediate risk and is consistent with protecting Good without overtly revealing Merlin-specific knowledge.
Player 1: Two successful quests with teams [1,2] and [0,1,2] make players 1 and 2 look relatively good, while 0 is also somewhat cleared; player 3 is less tested. As a Good servant on team voting, the safest play is to reject a 2-person quest 3 team that excludes two previously successful members, since quest 3 in 5-player Avalon requires 2 players and we have stronger data on 0/1/2 than on 3. Rejecting preserves information quality and avoids giving an untested player unnecessary influence.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 1 and 2 were both on successful quests, which gives them some good credibility, though one evil can still hide by passing. Current phase is team voting on quest 3 with team [1,3]. As evil Assassin on a 2-player quest, if this team goes I can choose fail and immediately give evil a point while making player 1 look less clean. Approving helps create a sabotage opportunity; rejecting only delays and may lead to an all-good team. My vote itself reveals little because evil may approve teams containing themselves.
Player 4: I am Minion with teammate 3. Proposed team [1,3] includes one evil (3) and one good-looking player (1, on both successful quests). On a 2-player quest, a single fail from 3 would cause the first evil point if approved. As evil, approving a team with my teammate on it is strong because it gives us direct influence and potential to get on the board. My vote also doesn't reveal much since evil often supports teams containing evil members.
Player 0: Two successful quests give mild good signals to players 1 and 2, especially since both prior teams containing them had 0 fails. Player 3 is less confirmed. As a Servant, I want to minimize risk on quest 3 by preferring proven players. This proposed team [1,3] drops both me and player 2 from the all-successful previous team and introduces 3 with less evidence, so rejecting preserves pressure for a safer team like including proven members 1 and 2. My reject gives little harmful info and supports good's caution.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know player 4 is evil. The proposed team [1,4] contains a known evil, so from a good-team perspective it must be rejected. Since we already have two successful quests, approving a team with known evil risks handing Evil a point and also gives no useful cover worth that cost. Rejecting is the optimal vote and aligns with prior successful teams that excluded 4.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Two quests have succeeded with teams [1,2] and [0,1,2], so players 0,1,2 look relatively good, while 3 and 4 are less cleared. Current proposed team [1,4] includes me and an untested player, and quest 3 in 5-player Avalon only needs 2 members, so this is risky compared with reusing proven pairs like [1,2]. Voting reject preserves pressure for a safer team and reveals skepticism of uncleared players, which helps Good more than approving a riskier composition.
Player 3: Players 1 and 2 were on two successful quests, so they look relatively good; teammate 4 is confirmed evil. On quest 3, a 2-person team of [1,4] gives evil a chance to put a fail and swing momentum, while still keeping suspicion somewhat on 1/4. As evil in team voting, I should generally support teams containing evil, especially my teammate, because we need a fail soon. Approving reveals some support for a team with 4, but the strategic value of getting evil onto the quest outweighs that.
Player 4: I am Minion with teammate 3. Proposed team [1,4] includes me and excludes known evil 3, so if approved I can choose fail on quest 3 and secure an evil point. Team [1,2] and [0,1,2] both succeeded with zero fails, making 1 and 2 somewhat good-leaning, though evil could have passed; still, from evil perspective this team is excellent because it guarantees one evil on mission without exposing both evils. Approving helps evil most. My vote reveals little since mixed voting patterns are normal.
Player 0: Two successful quests give strongest evidence that players 1 and 2 are good, since team [1,2] succeeded and [0,1,2] also succeeded with me good. Thus 1 is very likely good; 2 also looks good. Players 3 and 4 are less cleared. Current phase is team voting on a 2-person team [1,4]. As good, optimal play is to approve teams containing at least one strongly trusted player early enough when score is 2-0, especially since a success wins immediately for good. This team is not perfect compared to [1,2], but it is acceptable because 1 is highly trusted and if 4 is evil, evil may be forced to fail and expose themselves; if 4 is good, we win. My vote reveals I am willing to back a team anchored by a confirmed-good player rather than stall for an ideal lineup, which helps good more than rejecting without strong evidence against 4.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so the proposed team [0,1,2] excludes both known evil players. The prior fail came from team [1,4], confirming 4 is evil and leaving 1 likely good or at least better than including 3/4. In team voting on quest 4, approving a clean team of three goods is optimal and reveals little harmful information since rejecting would only cast doubt on a strong team.
Player 4: I know player 3 is evil with me. Quest 3 team [1,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, which strongly implicates me to attentive players but also leaves uncertainty on 1. Current proposed team [0,1,2] excludes both known evils, so as evil I should reject a likely all-good team that could succeed and put Good at 3-1. My vote reveals little since evil commonly reject good-looking teams; preserving the chance to stop this quest is worth it.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me. Team [0,1,2] excludes both evils, so if approved it is guaranteed to succeed and bring Good to 3 quest wins immediately. As evil on quest 4 at 2-1, the optimal play is to reject any all-good team. My vote signals mild suspicion, but preventing an auto-good success matters far more than concealment here.
Player 1: I’m Good (Servant). Team [1,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, so either 1 or 4 is evil; since I know I am good, 4 must be evil. Prior successful teams were [0,1,2] twice, which strongly suggests 0 and 2 are good. The proposed team [0,1,2] excludes the confirmed suspect 4 and matches the two prior successes. As Good, I should approve a team with the strongest success evidence. My vote also supports a clear information-consistent team and avoids helping evil sow doubt.
Player 0: As a Good Servant, I know Quest 3 failed with team [1,4], so at least one of 1 or 4 is Evil. Player 2 proposed [0,1,2] earlier and it succeeded with 0 fails, which makes 0,2 somewhat better, while 1 is mixed because Evil can pass. Current proposed team [0,1,2] excludes 4, the only player directly tied to a failed 2-person quest besides 1. For a 3-person quest requiring only one fail to lose, this is a reasonable team and likely better than alternatives including 4 or uncertain 3. Approving reveals limited info, but if it fails, suspicion sharpens on 1 versus 2 since I know I am Good.

experiences.intentions: Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me, so Merlin is among 0/1/2. Quest 3 failed on team [1,4], which strongly implicates 4 publicly and makes player 1 look relatively good from Merlin’s perspective if 4 chose to fail. Successful teams were [0,1,2] and [1,3] with me passing on [1,3], so player 1 appears on three successful quests and one failed quest with evil 4, making 1 the most central trusted good. Merlin likely steered trust around 1 and avoided overtly accusing both evils. As Assassin, optimal play is to pick the player most likely to be Merlin among the goods. Information reveal no longer matters beyond maximizing hit chance. Best guess: player 1 is Merlin.
